ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for tuna puff; mapped to closest parent class 'pastry food product' (FOODON_03302571). A more specific tuna-filled puff pastry class does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A03SJ (Pastries and similar products) is the closest defensible match. No specific tuna-filled puff pastry code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for tuna puff pastry as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One tuna puff typically weighs approximately 80 g and constitutes a single snack serving as commonly sold at Singaporean bakeries and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Tuna Puff — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tuna Puff (cr_id F11110029). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central puff pastry composition data (USDA SR Legacy #18337) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for tuna puff in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 59 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: savoury pastry / meat pie (GI ~55–65, medium range) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 59 × 21.4 / 100 ≈ 12.6 (serving size 80 g, carbohydrate 26.7 g/100 g × 0.8 = 21.4 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 410 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; high saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit tuna puff consumption due to sodium content of 410 mg/100 g and high saturated fat; choose low-sodium canned tuna, reduce portion size, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined puff pastry carbohydrates (26.7 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.4 g/100 g) and medium GI (~59) promote rapid postprandial glucose rise; high fat content may worsen insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should treat tuna puff as an occasional food only, limiting portion to half a puff, pairing with a high-fibre side, and monitoring postprandial glucose given the medium GI and low fibre content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) and estimated trans fat (~0.5 g/100 g) from butter-based puff pastry elevate LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk; sodium 410 mg/100 g further burdens vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit tuna puff due to high saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g), estimated trans fat (~0.5 g/100 g), and elevated sodium (410 mg/100 g), all of which adversely affect lipid profiles and blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tuna puff is a popular Singaporean baked snack consisting of a flaky, buttery puff pastry shell encasing a savoury filling of canned tuna and diced onion. The pastry is made from laminated dough with multiple layers of butter, producing a light, crisp, and golden exterior that shatters on biting. The filling is moist and mildly seasoned, combining the umami of canned tuna with the sweetness of cooked onion. Tuna puffs are widely sold at Singaporean bakeries, coffee shops, and hawker stalls as a breakfast or mid-morning snack. Nutritionally, tuna puff is energy-dense at 417 kcal/100 g, with high total fat (30.2 g/100 g) dominated by saturated fat (~12.5 g/100 g) from the butter-based pastry, and an estimated trans fat content of ~0.5 g/100 g from pastry shortening. Carbohydrate content is 26.7 g/100 g with only 1.4 g/100 g dietary fibre, resulting in a medium glycemic index (~59) and moderate glycemic load per serving (~12.6 for an 80 g puff). Sodium is elevated at 410 mg/100 g, driven by canned tuna, added salt, and pastry ingredients. Protein contributes 9.4 g/100 g from the tuna filling. Clinically, tuna puff is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, type 2 diabetes, or cardiovascular disease due to its high saturated fat, trans fat, sodium, and refined carbohydrate profile. Allergens include gluten, wheat, fish, and dairy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a tuna puff?
Is tuna puff high in sodium?
What is a tuna puff made of?
Is tuna puff suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11110029) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy puff pastry data as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this field is therefore medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Overall record confidence is high given the primary HPB source for all key macros.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (12.5 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.5 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #18337 (puff pastry, frozen, ready-to-bake) as the HPB Foodo database does not separately report saturated fat or trans fat for this item. These values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific tuna puff preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. GI of 59 is an analogous estimate based on savoury pastry / meat pie data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for tuna puff exists in any approved source. 4. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singaporean bakery preparation using halal-certified ingredients; this should be verified against the specific vendor's halal certification.
